questions.question: Explain the difference between the dot (.) operator and arrow (->) operator for accessing structure members. Give an example of each.
keyPoints: Dot operator accesses members using structure variable directly
Arrow operator accesses members using pointer to structure
Example with both: struct Student s; struct Student *p = &s; s.name = "Amit"; p->age = 20;

questions.question: What is a self-referential structure? How is it used in creating linked lists? Explain with a code example.
keyPoints: Structure containing pointer to same type
Used to create nodes of linked list
Example: struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; Each node points to next node

questions.question: Write a C program to dynamically allocate memory for a structure variable representing a Book (with title, author, price) and display its contents.
keyPoints: Use malloc() with sizeof(struct Book)
Check for NULL after allocation
Initialize members using arrow operator
Display using printf with -> operator
Free memory after use

questions.question: Explain the concept of passing structures to functions. Differentiate between pass by value and pass by reference with respect to their effect on the original structure.
keyPoints: Pass by value: creates copy, original unchanged, more memory
Pass by reference: passes address via pointer, can modify original
Use & operator when calling for pass by reference
Arrow operator used inside function to access members

questions.question: What is dynamic memory allocation for structures? How does malloc() differ from calloc() when allocating memory for structure arrays?
keyPoints: malloc(): allocates single block, memory contains garbage values
calloc(): allocates array, initializes all bytes to zero
malloc(bytes): takes total bytes
calloc(n, size): takes number of elements and element size

questions.question: Explain what is meant by structure padding. How does it affect the size of a structure? Can pointers to structures be affected by padding?
keyPoints: Compiler adds bytes for memory alignment
Structure size may be larger than sum of members
Padding is internal, not visible to programmer
Pointers store addresses, unaffected by internal padding

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer? How can it be avoided when working with dynamic memory allocation for structures?
keyPoints: Dangling pointer references freed memory
Occurs when memory is freed but pointer still holds old address
Prevention: set pointer to NULL after free()
Always check NULL before accessing freed pointer

questions.question: Write a complete C program to create a singly linked list with n nodes. Each node should store student information (roll number, name, marks). Implement functions to: (a) create list (b) display list (c) count total nodes (d) search a student by roll number.
keyPoints: Define structure with self-referential pointer
Use malloc() for node allocation
Implement create: loop for n nodes, link previous to new
Implement display: traverse using temp pointer until NULL
Implement count: traverse and increment counter
Implement search: compare roll numbers, return node if found
Free all nodes at end using free()

questions.question: Explain the following with respect to pointers and structures in C: (a) Arrow operator and its equivalence to dot operator (b) Array of pointers to structures (c) Pointer to array of structures (d) Constant pointer to structure. Illustrate each with a code example.
keyPoints: Arrow: (*ptr).member is equivalent to ptr->member
Array of pointers: struct Student *arr[10]; each element pointer
Pointer to array: struct Student *ptr = arrayName; pointer arithmetic
Constant pointer: const struct Student *p (cannot modify), struct Student *const p (cannot change address)

questions.question: Write a C program that demonstrates the use of typedef with structures. Show how typedef simplifies declaration of structure variables and pointers. Also explain the advantages of using typedef.
keyPoints: typedef creates alias: typedef struct { } Student;
Declare variables: Student s1, s2;
Declare pointers: Student *ptr;
Advantages: reduces typing, improves readability, hides implementation details
Example demonstrating creation, initialization, and pointer usage

questions.question: Create a structure to represent a complex number. Write functions to: (a) add two complex numbers (pass structures by reference) (b) multiply two complex numbers (c) display result. Demonstrate the program with example calls.
keyPoints: Define struct Complex with real and imaginary parts
Function for addition: (a+bi) + (c+di) = (a+c) + (b+d)i
Function for multiplication: (a+bi)*(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i
Pass structures by reference using pointers
Use arrow operator to access members inside functions
Print in format: a + bi
